coredata.dc:title: Investigate on the dry friction tribological behaviors and mechanism of carbon quantum dot/polyimide composites coating
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Enzhu
Su, Enhao
Subedi, Ayush
Liu, Shusheng
Wang, Jianping
Zhong, Hua
Hu, Kunhong
coredata.dc:description: The comprehensive utilization of biomass resources is to be prepared into functional nanomaterial. This paper prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using citric acid. A new CQDs/polyimide composites (CQDs/PI) coating fabricated on the surface of steel disks, has been widely used in bearing sleeve material or coatings in marine equipment parts and aerospace industries. Tribological properties of coating materials were investigated at different loads and reciprocating speeds of 10 mm/s under dry sliding conditions using a UMT-2 tribometer and compared to those of commercial MoS2 particles in PI composites. Scanning electron microscopy accompanied by energy spectroscopy (SEM/EDS) and 3D laser scanning microscopy were used to characterize the morphologies of the abrasion marks to clarify the tribological mechanisms. Results show that different levels of CQDs can improve composite coatings' anti-wear and friction reduction properties. The tribological mechanisms of CQDs were attributed to the enhancement of the hardness of the polyimide coatings by the CQDs and the formation of a transfer film on the surface of the ceramic balls during the friction process reducing the direct contact at the friction sub-interface.1
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sliding friction
Hardness
Carbon quantum dots
Solid lubricants
Surface analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to express their thanks to Mrs Yanjie Chen, and Mr. Zhixiang Li for their assistance in using the SEM/EDS and HRTEM images. This work was supported by National Nature Foundation of China ( 52075144 ) and Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number: 2008085ME167 ), Anhui University Outstanding Young Talents Programs (grant number: gxyqZD2020051 ) are gratefully acknowledged. The authors wish to express their thanks to Mrs Yanjie Chen, and Mr. Zhixiang Li for their assistance in using the SEM/EDS and HRTEM images. This work was supported by National Nature Foundation of China (52075144) and Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number: 2008085ME167), Anhui University Outstanding Young Talents Programs (grant number: gxyqZD2020051) are gratefully acknowledged. ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of friction pair and wear measurement.
FETEM image of carbon quantum dots.
(a) Particle size distribution of CQDs (b) X-ray diffraction pattern of CQDs.
(a) Thermo-gravimetric curve and (b) Hardness of polyimide and its composites.
Coating adhesion test (3D laser loading force: 80 N).
Average friction coefficients (a) and average wear rates (b) of PI and PI inclusing CQDs or MoS2 at a sliding speed of 10 mm/s and under different loading conditions.
Average friction coefficients (a) and average wear rates (b) of CQD/MoS2 in PI at a sliding speed of 10 mm/s and under different loading conditions.
Surface abrasion profile and wear area of the sample under a load of 25 N (For time 30 min).
SEM images of wear traces on the surfaces of the upper specimen balls.
Diagrams of formation process and wear mechanism of films.
EDS analysis of wear spot area under the load of 80 N.

head.ce:title: Investigate on the dry friction tribological behaviors and mechanism of carbon quantum dot/polyimide composites coating

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, 99 Jinxiu Road, Hefei, 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, 99 Jinxiu Road, Hefei, 230601, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The comprehensive utilization of biomass resources is to be prepared into functional nanomaterial. This paper prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using citric acid. A new CQDs/polyimide composites (CQDs/PI) coating fabricated on the surface of steel disks, has been widely used in bearing sleeve material or coatings in marine equipment parts and aerospace industries. Tribological properties of coating materials were investigated at different loads and reciprocating speeds of 10 mm/s under dry sliding conditions using a UMT-2 tribometer and compared to those of commercial MoS2 particles in PI composites. Scanning electron microscopy accompanied by energy spectroscopy (SEM/EDS) and 3D laser scanning microscopy were used to characterize the morphologies of the abrasion marks to clarify the tribological mechanisms. Results show that different levels of CQDs can improve composite coatings' anti-wear and friction reduction properties. The tribological mechanisms of CQDs were attributed to the enhancement of the hardness of the polyimide coatings by the CQDs and the formation of a transfer film on the surface of the ceramic balls during the friction process reducing the direct contact at the friction sub-interface.1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the novel carbon nanoparticles with particle size less than 10 nm discovered in recent years [1], which are low cost, environmentally friendly, less toxic, and show great potential in many applications such as sensors [2], medicines [3], photovoltaic devices [4], biology [5], and tribology [6]. With further research, great progress has been made in the preparation of carbon quantum dots and improvement of lubrication performance for lubricants. Currently, CQDs are prepared by using biomass [1,7–9] resources as the carbon source such as the watermelon rind [10], citric acid [11], chili pepper [12], etc. Compared with the traditional methods, the utilization of biomass resources as precursors is more economical and sustainable than non-renewable carbon-based materials, which broadens the scope of utilization of biomass resources to a certain extent. In addition, the research on CQDs as lubrication additives in base oils has been fruitful [13–17]. Xiao [18] successfully prepared sulfur-doped biomass carbon quantum dots using a one-pot hydrothermal method and investigated the effect of the particles on the lubricating properties of aqueous lubricants. The findings show that sulfur-doped CQDs have excellent tribological properties. Under high shear stress, CQDs exhibit a rolling effect or interlayer sliding behavior, thereby reducing the coefficient of friction. Tang [19] used CQDs as an aqueous lubrication improver and found that the frictional wear performance of aqueous-based lubricants could be reduced by 33% and 80%, respectively, when the CQDs content was 0.1 wt%. The mechanism is attributed to the nano-bearing and nano-filling mechanism of CQDs. Huang [20] stated that CQDs/CuSx nanocomposite particles exhibit high chemical activity and stability when used as liquid paraffin additives, and enhance lubrication and repair metal wear surfaces. Wang [21] prepared ionic liquid carbon dots by one-pot pyrolysis and anion exchange reactions, which exhibited good dispersion stability after the addition of polyethylene glycol (PEG) base oil. The coefficient of friction and wear diameter were reduced by 70% and 33%, respectively, at the test condition of 392 N due to the synergistic lubrication effect of carbon core and surface ionic liquid. The above studies have indicated that CQDs or their composites can significantly alter the lubricity properties of lubricants.

As we all know, with the accelerated development of industry, the stability and reliability of machinery and equipment become more and more critical [22–24]. However, machinery and equipment exist in special working conditions, such as marine equipment parts and aerospace industry applications, which need to face high and low temperatures, vacuum, radiation and corrosion environment, etc [25–27]. The lubrication of metallic materials is difficult to achieve or difficult to maintain, and there may even be problems such as contamination damage to equipment caused by lubricant leakage. Therefore, it is particularly urgent to develop solid lubricating materials that reduce frictional wear of moving parts [28]. Polyimide, a type of polymeric material, has gained wide attention in the field of tribology due to its excellent properties [29–32], including high strength, light weight, excellent thermal stability, anti-wear and anti-corrosion properties [33]. However, pure polyimide still exhibits a high coefficient of friction [34]. To overcome this problem, the tribological properties of polyimide can be significantly improved by introducing reinforcing fibers [35], solid lubricants [36] or functional nanomaterials [37] into polyimide polymers. Ye [38] et al. employed amino-modified fluorinated graphene (AFG) as a nano-filler for polyimide (PI). The results showed that AFG significantly improved the thermal stability, mechanical properties and wear resistance of PI. The strong physical and chemical interactions between AFG and PI matrix enhance the interfacial compatibility of PI/AFG composites and better transfer of stress and heat, thus improving the mechanical and tribological properties of PI. Qi [39] et al. investigated the effect of fullerene carbon (C60) on the tribological properties of polyimide (PI) composites under different temperature conditions. The results show that the addition of 1.0 wt% of C60 can dramatically enhance the tribological properties of polyimide composites. At -100 °C/200 °C, the coefficient of friction and wear rate were reduced by 86.4%/50.0% and 28.6%/30.1%, respectively, indicating that the introduction of C60 can improve the lubrication and load-bearing capacity of the sliding interface. Wan [40] et al. developed NG/PI composites by compounding nano-graphite (NG) as a lubricant additive with polyimide through a hot compression molding process, and investigated the dry friction behavior of the composites and compared them with pure PI. The performance indicated that the friction coefficient of the composite with 5 wt% NG filler was reduced by 55.63% compared to the pure PI, and the wear rate was reduced by up to 39%.

In summary, CQDs have been widely reported in the field of lubricants, but less in the field of polymeric materials, especially in polyimide. The small size of carbon quantum dots, compared to conventional functional additives for polyimide, has the potential to solve the problem of dispersion in polyimide. Therefore, in this paper, carbon quantum dots were successfully prepared by a simple hydrothermal synthesis method and applied to polyimide as a functional improver to systematically investigate the dry tribological behavior of the composites and their tribological wear mechanisms, and to compare them with the conventional solid MoS2 particles. Molybdenum disulfide was selected to further enhance the anti-wear and friction reduction function of the composites, and to develop CQDs composites with excellent performance. The successful implementation of this technology will help to realize the comprehensive utilization of biomass resources, develop good green lubricating materials, and lay a certain foundation for carbon material tribology.

Citric acid monohydrate as a biomass substitute, N, N-dimethylacetamide, all purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pyromellitic dianhydride and 4, 4′-diaminodiphenyl ether were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N-aminoethyl 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (KH792), purchased from Hubei Wuda Silicone Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water is prepared by laboratory ultrapure water.

Preparation of CQDs: CQDs were prepared experimentally using the hydrothermal method [41]. In a beaker of 30 mL of deionized water, 1 g of citric acid monohydrate was slowly added, and magnetically stirred to dissolve completely, then 30 mL of KH792 solution was slowly added, magnetically stirred for 5 min, mixed well, and placed in a PTFE liner in a reaction kettle in a constant temperature drying oven (180 °C) for 5 h. The reaction time was 5 h. The mixture was dialyzed for 48 h (dialysis bag: 3500D), removed by rotary evaporation to about 10 mL, frozen in a refrigerator, and then freeze-dried for 72 h to obtain a biomass carbon quantum dots powder.

Preparation of CQDs/PI or MoS
2
/PI composites: The citric acid carbon quantum dot composites were prepared using a two-step method. Firstly, 35 mL of N, N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC) was weighed into a three-necked flask, 2 g of 4,4′-diamino diphenyl ether (ODA) and carbon citrate quantum dots and MoS2 were added and then sonicated until all the ODA was dissolved and the carbon quantum dots and molybdenum disulfide were well dispersed. Afterward, 2.18 g of pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA) was added and the reaction was stirred thoroughly for 24 h at low temperature (below 5 °C) under a nitrogen atmosphere, at the end of which the experiment was completed to obtain polyamide acid (PAA) with a solid content of 11.3%. The prepared PAA solution was weighed and placed 0.65 g on the surface of a smoothly polished GCr15 steel disc, coated with a tool, and heated horizontally in a vacuum oven to 80 °C for 8 h to allow the solvent to evaporate as much as possible. After that, the CQDs-PAA was held in a drying oven for 2 h at 100, 200, and 300 °C to imidate CQDs to CQDs-PI, yielding 0.1 wt% CQDs/PI, 0.25 wt% CQDs/PI, 0.5 wt% CQDs/PI and 1.0 wt% CQDs/PI, 0.1 wt% MoS2/PI, 0.25 wt% MoS2/PI, 0.1 wt% (CQDs-MoS2)/PI, 0.25 wt% (CQDs-MoS2)/PI, 0.5 wt% (CQDs-MoS2)/PI, 1 wt% (CQDs-MoS2)/PI composites. For all of PI including the CQD/MoS2 samples, the addition rate of CQDs and MoS2 is 1:1.

The tribological properties of the prepared polyimide and its composites were investigated using an UMT-2 tribometer. The friction substrate was a ball-disc contact (see Fig. 1 ) with a 6.0 mm diameter ceramic ball as the upper specimen and a GCr15 (Size: φ28.0 mm × 2.0 mm) as the lower specimen disc with a carbon quantum dot/polyimide composite and a pure polyimide surface. In order to meet the conditions for the use of bearing bush in different occasions, the experimental conditions are set as follows: the friction test lasted 30 min in an air environment, the loading forces were 15 N, 25 N, and 35 N, the reciprocating speed was 10 mm/s, the single wear distance was 5 mm and each group was tested at least three times to minimize the test error. After the friction test, the wear marks of the specimen are observed under a 3D laser scanning microscope (VK-X100) and the wear area (S0) is calculated and then the wear volume (V0) is calculated from the length of the wear marks. The wear resistance of the material is then evaluated by the calculated wear rate, which is calculated as follows.
K 0
=
V 0
/
( L × N )
K0: Wear rate, (m3/Nm), V0; Average wear area volume, μm3, L: Sliding distance, m, N: Applied load, N
Finally, SEM/EDS (SU0810) was used to observe the wear points of the friction surface on the specimen ball, to observe the morphology and to analyze the friction mechanism.

The morphology and structure of the samples were characterized using field emission projection electron microscopy (FETEM, JEM-2100F), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FI-IR, NicoletiS5o), and X-ray diffractometer (X-ray, TD-3500). The thermal stability of the samples was determined using a thermal weight loss analyzer (TG, 0209F1) with a N2 atmosphere and a temperature rise rate of 10 °C/min. The adhesion and hardness of the composites were determined with a coating adhesion automatic scratch tester (WS-2005) and a shore hardness (HV) tester.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents a transmission electron microscope image of CQDs, from which it can be seen that the prepared CQDs particles have a spherical and homogeneous morphology. Fig. S1(a) shows the dispersion of CQDs in pure water, which illustrates that the CQDs are well dispersed in water with almost no undissolved CQDs powder. Fig. S1(b) illustrates the fluorescence diagram of CQDs, which demonstrates that the fluorescence of CQDs is very intense.

As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), the particle size of the CQDs was counted by transmission electron microscopy images and the prepared CQDs were distributed between 1 and 3.5 nm, with an average particle diameter of about 1.8 nm. Fig. 3 (b) shows the physical phase examination of CQDs using XRD. The material produced by the pyrolysis of citric acid with KH792 at 180 °C only has a broad peak centered at about 21°, demonstrating the presence of a small number of graphite micro-crystals, most of which belong to amorphous carbon, indicating that KH792 promoted the carbonization of citric acid to make the amorphous carbon product at 180 °C.

The absorption peaks in the infrared spectrogram are related to the structure of the molecular functional group and its vibrational form as shown in Fig. S2. The –CO stretching vibration peak at 1251 cm−1 in the carbon quantum dot indicates an acylation of the citric acid with the amino group in the silane coupling agent KH792.1005 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of Si–O–Si. The peaks at 3326 cm−1 and 737 cm−1 are the carbon chain backbone –CH2- stretching vibrations and antisymmetric deformation peaks, respectively. 2973 cm−1 is the binding peak of the –NH2 stretching vibrations.

Excellent thermal stability and mechanical properties are important for the use of polyimide composites in harsh environments. Fig. 4 (a) provides the thermal weight loss curves of PI and its composites. It can be seen that the addition of CQDs and MoS2 has almost no effect on the thermal stability of the polyimide materials, and their weight loss temperatures are all in the range of 500∼650 °C. Fig. 4 (b) shows the hardness of the PI and its composite coating, which shows that the hardness of the composite is gradually increasing with the increase in carbon quantum dots. The introduction of molybdenum disulfide, on the contrary, reduces the hardness of the composite, mainly due to the lower hardness of the molybdenum disulfide particles.

From Fig. 5 (a) it can be concluded that the pure PI has peeled off at about 57.9 N loadings, the area of detachment is large, and there is no obvious adhesion of small particles in the peeled-off area, indicating that the coating adhesion performance is poor. It is apparent that the adhesion properties of the composite coatings with the addition of both CQDs and MoS2 are superior to those of pure PI, and both improve the adhesion properties of PI. Among the composite coatings with the addition of CQDs, Fig. 5(b–e) the area of coating peeling with the increase of CQDs, the adhesion performance is increasing, and there are no obvious large pieces of peeling. The best coating adhesion was achieved at 1 wt% CQDs, with no peeling at 80 N loading. In contrast, the MoS2 composite coating Fig. 5(f, g) shows a small area of peeling at around 60 N, where Fig. 5(h–k) CQDs-MoS2 composite coating, the coating adhesion gradually decreases as the concentration of both increases. This indicates that the addition of carbon quantum dots and molybdenum disulfide blends occurs antagonistically. Nevertheless, in general, the improvement in adhesion properties of polyimide is preferable with carbon quantum dots.

Fig. 6 demonstrates the effect of varying load on the average coefficient of friction and wear rate for different additions of carbon quantum dots or molybdenum disulfide in PI at a reciprocating speed of 10 mm/s. As can be seen from Fig. 6(a), the friction reduction performance of the composites with CQDs or MoS2 added at a load of 25 N in a dry friction environment is better than that of pure polyimide. The average friction coefficient of pure polyimide is around 0.37, while the lowest average friction of 1 wt% CQDs/PI is around 0.24, which is a reduction of around 33.3% compared to that of pure polyimide. At the same tested conditions, the wear rate of pure PI is 4.75 × 10−5 mm3/(Nm) as shown in Fig. 6(b). The wear rate of PI including 1 wt% CQDs reduced with the 63%. Results indicated that the CQDs plays an important role in strengthening the friction reduction and wear resistance properties of PI including CQDs particles.

As is well known, the molybdenum disulfide itself has good friction-reducing properties and was added to the PI as a traditional lubricating additive as a reference. It was found that the coefficient of friction and wear rate did not vary significantly for different concentrations of MoS2 added. The reason is ascribed to the low hardness and dispersion property of MoS2 particles in PI resulting in 0.5 wt% and 1 wt% MoS2/PI composites have failed at 35 N load, so their average friction coefficients and wear rates are not available.

Fig. 7 demonstrates the effect of varying load on wear rate and average coefficient of friction for different additions of CQDs/MoS2 composites at a reciprocating speed of 10 mm/s. According to Fig. 7(a), for the composites with the addition of CQDs/MoS2 nanoparticles, the average friction coefficient of the composites reached a minimum value of 0.3 for the addition of 0.25 wt% CQDs/MoS2, a reduction of approximately 23.3%. At the same testing condition, wear rate of composite with addition of 0.25 wt% CQDs/MoS2 deduced to 2.75 × 10−5 mm3/(Nm) as shown in Fig. 7(b). Besides, 0.5 and 1 wt% CQDs/MoS2 composites have failed at 35 N load, so their average friction coefficients and wear rates are not available, which again prove that the MoS2 will cause the reduction of hardness for composites.

In all, combined with the results of the friction reduction and wear resistance performances described above, it can be obtained that 1 wt% CQDs can provide good anti-wear and friction reduction of PI at a load of 25 N and a reciprocating speed of 10 mm/s 0.25 wt% CQDs/MoS2 can provide good anti-wear and friction reduction of PI. CQDs particles provide a friction and wear reduction effect for PI composite materials.

Fig. 8 gives the area of wear marks and the 3D shape and wear area of the composite material under a load of 25 N. The composites with the addition of CQDs and MoS2 showed a large reduction in the depth and width of the surface abrasion. Fig. 8 (a) illustrate the surface profile and wear area of the pure PI. The depth and width of the wear marks are deeper and larger than those of the other composites and are attributed to friction-induced cyclic contact stresses leading to fatigue wear of the material. A large number of undulating furrows in the area of the abrasion marks indicates that a large number of abrasive chips were generated during the friction process, which aggravated the frictional damage on the PI surface, resulting in a large wear area of 4329 μm2. Fig. 8 (b ∼ e) show the surface topographies and wear areas of the composites with 0.1 wt% CQDs/PI, 0.25 wt% CQDs/PI, 0.5 wt% CQDs/PI and 1.0 wt% CQDs/PI. The tribological properties of the composites increased with the addition of CQDs as their concentration increased and their wear areas decreased, with the form of wear of the material changing from fatigue and abrasive wear to adhesive wear. As can be seen in Fig. 8(e), the 1 wt% CQDs/PI composite has narrower and shallower wear marks and therefore a smaller wear volume, with a wear area of 1229 μm2. In contrast, the wear areas of the composites with the addition of MoS2 in Fig. 8 (f, g) increased with increasing concentration, with a minimum wear rate of 1965 μm2 for 0.1 wt% MoS2/PI. This indicated that CQDs have better anti-wear properties than MoS2. From Fig. 8(h ∼ k) it can be noticed that the minimum wear area of the CQDs-MoS2/PI composite coating is 2288 μm2 in size at a concentration of 0.25 wt%, with little change in wear area compared to the addition of CQDs and MoS2 alone.

Table 1 displays the results of the EDS analysis of the wear spot area. From the table, surface elements of the wear spots for pure PI friction contain mainly C, N, O, and Si at 7.75 wt%, 33.64 wt%, 11.17 wt%, and 45.36 wt% respectively. When 1 wt% CQDs are added to PI for lubrication, the surface elements of wear spot obviously increase for C, N and O, and at 24.18 wt%, 42.18 wt%, 31.39 wt%, respectively. For Si element, the content decreases at 1.90 wt%. These results indicated the transfer film formed on the surface of balls resulting in the low friction coefficient and wear rate. At 0.25 wt% MoS2 added to PI for lubrication, the surface elements of wear spot contained mainly S and Mo at 1.23 wt%, and 1.30 wt%, respectively. It could be used to clarify the MoS2 to take part in formation of transfer film. When adding 0.1 wt% (CQDs/MoS2) to the polyimide for lubrication, the surface elements of wear spot contained mainly C, N, O, Si, S, and Mo at 14.02 wt%, 47.39 wt%, 35.02 wt%, 3.02 wt%, 0.28 wt%, and 0.26 wt% respectively. The outcome suggested that a certain amount of carbon quantum dots and molybdenum disulfide formed a transfer film in the area of the wear spot, thus providing an anti-wear and anti-friction effect.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots/polyimide composites were successfully prepared using citric acid, and both CQDs and MoS2 were able to improve the tribological properties of PI.

At low loads, the tribological properties of the composite are better at a CQDs mass fraction of 1 wt%, where the carbon quantum dots promote material transfer during the friction process. The generation of polymer transfer film can separate the direct contact between the matrix above and below the friction interface and improve the anti-wear properties of the composites.

At high loads, the tribological performance decreases as the mass fraction of CQDs added increases, and the tribological performance of the composite is better when the mass fraction of CQDs added is 0.1 wt%.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their thanks to Mrs Yanjie Chen, and Mr. Zhixiang Li for their assistance in using the SEM/EDS and HRTEM images. This work was supported by National Nature Foundation of China (52075144) and Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number: 2008085ME167), Anhui University Outstanding Young Talents Programs (grant number: gxyqZD2020051) are gratefully acknowledged. The Talent Research Fund of Hefei University (grant number: 21-22RC33) and Graduate Innovation and entrepreneurship program (grant number: 21YCXL37) and the Open Project of Anhui Province Engineering Laboratory of Intelligent Demolition Equipment (APELIDE2021B003) and Excellent Scientific Research and Innovation Team of Anhui University (2022AH010096) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wear.2023.205048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: a review
Mater. Today Chem.
